versions.line: Offline app shell. The board now installs a service worker that saves the page, styles, and scripts at their exact current version, so opening with no signal still renders the app frame (live data and the page itself stay network-first and refresh normally; tiles, radar, and APIs are untouched). When a new build is ready you get a small Update available notice with Reload and Later choices, in English and Spanish; nothing reloads on its own mid-session.

Visible compass on phones. The compass rose in the map controls was too small to read on mobile: the N label and the red north needle are now larger and bolder, the ring has stronger contrast, and the whole rose scales up to match the bigger phone buttons. Live-heading rotation works exactly as before.

Field usability polish. Buttons you tap in the field (feed actions, card actions, header controls) are now full 44px touch targets on phones, all five tabs stay visible on-screen in English and Spanish (Team and Resources no longer scroll away invisibly), and freshness and source text is larger and easier to read in glare. Also fixed: a river no longer shows as rising toward a crest that already passed.

Mobile legends and event accuracy. The radar, rainfall, inundation, and surge map legends now collapse to small pills on phones (tap to expand), so nothing covers the middle of the screen, and landscape phones are handled too. The public feed, crest summary, and history playback now follow the active event (TS Bertha) instead of carrying the prior Hill Country event, and record-crest context is live again for 216 coastal gauges. You can also add ResponderTX to your home screen as a branded app, and Drive Mode text now matches the brand palette.

Quality pass. The screen-wake hold is more reliable: a rare timing case could let the screen dim while you were sharing your location or using Drive Mode, and that is fixed. Radar tile retries no longer reload tiles that a radar refresh already replaced. And the Spanish interface now uses the formal “toque” wording consistently in the map flag tooltip, the tide table, and the compass controls.

New map layer: Hays County flood cams. It gathers the Hays County Office of Emergency Services flood cameras along the San Marcos corridor: Post Road and Little Arkansas Rd at the Blanco River, the FM150 Onion Creek double crossing (north and south), and the two Upper San Marcos NRCS dams. Find it under the flood cameras group in the map layers sheet; it is off by default, and tapping a camera opens the latest still with its capture time, marked stale if the image gets old, just like the other camera sources. Each camera is checked for a live picture when the camera list is rebuilt, so an offline camera is left out instead of showing a broken image.

New for live teams: the team map is now interactive. Tap any teammate (or yourself) to open a popup with their handle, status, role or specialty, how long since they were last seen, and their GPS accuracy, so you can read a unit without opening the roster. You can also place a shared team marker exactly where you want it: tap the drop button to arm a 'tap the map to place' mode, shown with a crosshair and a hint banner, then tap the spot to open the marker form there; a 'Use map center' button keeps the old one-tap center drop, and the mode cancels itself on Escape, when the marker form opens, or when you switch away from the app. Your own location now draws a GPS accuracy ring on the map, and the exact accuracy in meters and feet shows in the popups, so you always know how precise a fix is.

New: your screen now stays awake while you are actively sharing your location with a team or using Drive Mode, so your phone does not dim and lock in the middle of a response. The screen-awake hold lets go the moment you stop sharing, leave the team, close Drive Mode, or switch away from the tab, and it comes back when you return, so it never keeps the screen lit in the background and drains your battery. It needs the secure site (the https address) and a browser that supports screen wake locks; where that is not available, such as the plain http LAN board or an older iPhone, everything works as before and the screen just follows your normal auto-lock.

Fix: some radar and forecast cells could stay blank after you switched the layer on. Turning it on used to load every animation frame at the same moment, so if the busy radar server dropped a tile there was no way to get it back and you were left with holes. Now the layer loads the visible 'now' frame first and adds the older frames a moment later, and any tile that fails to load is retried a few times, so the cells fill in instead of leaving gaps. The forecast layer also draws at a lighter resolution that loads faster and more reliably from the slower model server.

New: the compass button now shows your live heading. Tap it and the compass rose spins so its red north needle keeps pointing at true north as you turn your phone, an easy way to line up the map with what is in front of you. Tap it again to lock it back to a plain north-up compass. This is an extra that only turns on where your device and browser support it: on iPhones it asks once for motion permission, and on desktops or unsupported browsers the compass simply stays north up as before, never blank or broken.

New: a 'Storm surge risk' map layer you can switch on from the Tropical group in the layer picker. It draws NOAA's National Hurricane Center storm surge risk maps (the SLOSH 'Maximum of MEOWs' product) for a near worst-case Category 5 hurricane, shading how deep surge water could get above ground: red for greater than 9 feet, orange for greater than 6, yellow for greater than 3, and blue for less than 3, with levee areas flagged to check with local officials. It is a planning map of what is possible, not a live forecast, so it stays available even when no storm is active. It sits at half opacity so roads and the basemap still read through it, and it quietly shows a small 'unavailable' note if the map service cannot be reached.

New during Tropical Storm Bertha: a 'Coastal water levels' card in the Resources tab shows storm surge at NOAA tide stations along the upper and central Texas coast, from Sabine Pass down to Aransas Pass. For each station it lists the current water level and, most usefully, the surge residual: how far the observed water sits above (or below) the tide that was predicted for that moment, with a rising or falling arrow. Stations are sorted worst first so the hardest-hit float to the top, each with the reading time and a link to NOAA Tides & Currents. It loads only when you open Resources, refreshes while you watch, and quietly shows 'unavailable' for any station that is briefly offline.

The north compass now sits directly below the Share button in the top-right corner and matches its size and look, so the two read as one tidy control pair (it used to be a larger, separate compass floating in the bottom-right). It is now a clean north-up indicator; the tap that did nothing has been removed.

When there is an active tropical threat to Texas, the storm now shows automatically. The tropical tracker (forecast cone, track, and watches/warnings) turns itself on the first time the Texas alert feed carries an active Storm Surge, Tropical Storm, or Hurricane Warning or Watch, so you do not have to enable it by hand during an event. If you turn it off it stays off, and it remains an opt-in layer when no tropical threat is active.

The Social tab is now folded into Resources, so everything you consult or share lives in one place. Resources opens with a live status of the board's data feeds: the seven sources (alerts, gauges, roads, then forecast, USGS, reports, and board data), most important first, each shown green when fresh, amber when aging, or grey when stale, with how long ago it updated and a countdown to the next refresh. Below that comes routing (low-water crossings and recently reopened roads), then shelters and hotlines, then two tidy drop-downs that stay closed until you need them: 'Monitor / verify' for the live social searches and scanner feeds, and 'Recovery & donations' for rebuild funds and aid. The clutter is gone: news live-blogs and links that just duplicated the map's own radar and gauge layers were removed, leaving the most actionable resources. Old links to the Social tab now open Resources.

A tropical cyclone tracker is now available as a map layer (off by default; turn it on under the new Tropical group in the layers menu). When a storm is active it shows the National Hurricane Center forecast cone, the forecast and past track, the current and forecast storm positions, and the coastal watch and warning lines, colored by type so you can tell a hurricane warning from a tropical storm or storm surge watch at a glance. Tap any part of it for the storm name, category, top winds, movement, and advisory time. The data comes from NOAA's National Hurricane Center. When no storms are active it simply shows nothing. Also, on phones the radar and forecast legend no longer stretches across the middle of the screen; it now sits as a small chip in the bottom-right corner.

Coastal response for Tropical Storm Bertha. The board now covers the upper and mid Texas coast (Matagorda to Sabine, inland to Houston and Beaumont) instead of the Hill Country, so it opens on the coast and shows river gauges, road closures, and low-water crossings there. And a real gap is fixed: Storm Surge, Tropical Storm, Hurricane, and High Wind warnings were being dropped because the alert feed was filtered to the word "flood," so the most dangerous coastal warnings never appeared; they now show. Coverage reverts to the Hill Country once Bertha clears.

If you use the live team feature on your phone, switching apps or locking your screen no longer drops you from the team: you keep your spot, your color, and your breadcrumb trail instead of coming back as a brand-new blank member. When a teammate drops off the map, either because they lose signal long enough for the server to drop them or because they leave, command now keeps a faded 'last known position' marker with the time, and the roster clearly flags them as lost contact, instead of the person simply vanishing. Team messages such as 'location unavailable', 'this team has expired', 'reconnected', and 'you left the team' now appear in their own notice line in the Team tab that stays put until you dismiss it, instead of flashing once and being overwritten by the refresh clock. The location-denied warning is especially sticky because it means you are not actually sharing your location.

Your team status now does something real: setting yourself Unavailable stops sharing your live location but keeps you on the team, so your crew still sees the roster and you can flip back to In field, Standby, or Rehab to start sharing again. Leaving the team is now a separate button so the two are never confused. The status control is clearer too: it labels which status you are in with a check and a pressed look, tints each button with its status color, and explains the difference between going Unavailable and leaving. Selected buttons in the team dialogs now use white text so they stay readable on the dark background.

Accessibility improvements for keyboard and screen-reader users. Every pop-up dialog now keeps keyboard focus inside it while it is open, moves focus into the dialog when it opens, and returns focus to whatever you were on when it closes; the rest of the page is held inert so you cannot tab out into it by accident. Escape now closes the alert reader, the team marker and role dialogs, and the field notes panel, which previously had no keyboard way to close. The 911 safety notice keeps its single, deliberate close path: it traps focus on its acknowledgment button and never closes on Escape or an outside click, so you always confirm you have read it.

Radar and the HRRR forecast are now a single "Radar & forecast" layer with one timeline you scrub from the observed past through NOW out to +12 hours, plus a shared reflectivity legend that tells you whether you are looking at observed radar or the forecast model. Pressing play on the forecast now animates smoothly instead of freezing on the first hour, and turning the layer on while radar is already showing keeps you on the live picture at NOW. The forecast now reaches +12h (it was +18h) so every projected hour stays on one consistent model run.

Two team fixes. Creating or joining a live team now works from the LAN board, not only the public site (it was failing with "Could not create the team."). And the "Create team" button text is now white so it stays readable on the dark accent, instead of near-black text that was hard to see on a dark background.

A small data-accuracy fix. When a river gauge's source has no current reading (a sensor briefly offline), the board now says "no current reading" instead of showing a placeholder "-999 ft". These gauges were already marked stale and left out of the flood and crest calculations, so nothing about the safety picture changes; a gauge with a real but simply old reading still shows its last value.

Internal cleanup only. We removed some dead code and out-of-date notes in the source and merged a few duplicated pieces of logic into shared helpers. Nothing about how the board looks or works has changed.

Team members no longer vanish when their phone screen locks or they switch apps. Sharing simply pauses, their last position stays on the command map, and it resumes on its own when they come back, instead of the whole team losing them the moment the screen goes dark. The 911 do-not-self-deploy safety notice is now firmer too: it closes only when you tap the acknowledgment button, not on Escape or a stray tap. Behind the scenes, the local command oversight view is now limited to devices on your own network and its access token is no longer accepted from a web link, so it cannot leak.

The tab with the live searches, scanners, and road and crossing status is called Social again instead of Monitor, and it now sits at the far right of the tab bar. Only its name and place changed. What is inside it is the same, and any link or saved view you already shared still opens the same tab.

The bottom-center re-center button is gone. The locate button still centers on you and turns following back on when you tap it, and now a small hint slides out next to it the moment you pan off follow, flashes a few times to point you back to that button, then tucks away on its own. The YOU marker also pulses just a few times each time you tap locate or re-center, then settles into a steady dot instead of pulsing the whole time; live tracking keeps it moving without starting the pulse over.

The camera layers are now organized into a Cameras section with three collapsible groups: Flood / low-water first, then Traffic, then Border. The long flat list of seven camera sources no longer fills the layer panel. Each group starts collapsed and opens with one tap, and a group opens on its own whenever a camera in it is already on, so a shared link or active camera is never hidden. Each open group also shows how many of its cameras are on.

The tab that was called Social is now called Monitor, because that is what it is for: live searches, scanners, and now road and low-water crossing status to check before you route. Resources is now a clean help directory of shelters, hotlines, trusted data, and subscribe links. Each of the two tabs has a short line under its title explaining what it is for, in English and Spanish, and any link or saved view you already shared still opens the same tab.

Smoother, continuous follow tracking. When the map is following you, it now takes a live location feed and glides toward each new GPS fix at a steady pace, instead of jumping once every 10 seconds, so driving feels like one fluid motion the way a nav app does. Tapping locate or re-center still snaps you in, and panning the map still turns following off.

Smoother location tracking. When the map is following you, it now glides to each new GPS fix instead of snapping across the distance, which looks much better while driving, and it updates every 10 seconds instead of 30 so the steps are smaller. Follow only runs after you tap locate or re-center, and panning the map still turns it off.

Internal cleanup. We removed an unused archival-photo overlay that was part of historical playback but had no data behind it, so it never appeared on the map. Nothing you use changes: the event timeline and radar playback work exactly as before.

A team reliability fix. If you background the app or switch away on your phone, you used to silently drop off your team; now the app reconnects you automatically when you return, restoring your handle, role, and profile and resuming location sharing. Leaving a team on purpose is also more reliable now. An internal cleanup of the team relay code rounds it out, with no change to how anything works.

Location tools leveled up. Tap Locate or Re-center on me and the map now follows you, staying centered as you move so you do not drift off-screen; pan or pinch-zoom yourself to stop following. Locate now zooms to the same closer level as Re-center. A new compass in the bottom-right corner shows which way is north.

A logo tweak. The ResponderTX wordmark in the header is about 20 percent larger so the name reads more clearly; the Texas-shape part of the logo stays the same size. Light and dark both updated. Nothing else changed.

After you tap Locate and allow location, your position now keeps updating on the map about every 30 seconds, in both the normal app and Drive Mode. The map no longer jumps back to you on those updates, so if you pan away it stays where you put it. A new ‘Re-center on me’ button sits at the center-bottom of the map and highlights when your marker scrolls off-screen; tap it to jump back to your current location. Available in English and Spanish.

Three additions to the live team feature. Crew can now set a Rehab (rest) status, in addition to in field, standby, and unavailable. When you drop a shared marker on the map you can assign it to a specific teammate, whose name then shows on that marker. And when you create a team you can save your current feed filters as a preset, so everyone who joins opens with the same view. Everything is available in English and Spanish.

Small correctness and polish fixes. The browser tab title and social-share cards now read "Live Hazard Awareness" too, matching the header (the "for Texas" was still lingering there). A team K9-name input that had lost its styling is fixed, a couple of blank-value placeholders and one camera label were cleaned up, and the SITREP button's copied message now translates. Nothing about how the board works has changed.

Added a new camera layer for the City of Arlington, in the Dallas and Fort Worth area, with about 60 live traffic cameras at street intersections. It is its own layer that stays off until you turn it on, and it shows up in the camera viewer, in Drive Mode's nearest-camera list, in the layer menu, and in shared links, in both English and Spanish. We also fixed the tool that refreshes the camera list so it works again, and refreshed the full list at the same time (the Houston camera set grew).

The SITREP button now does two things at once, on both computer and phone: it copies the situation report to your clipboard as before, and it also opens a clean, easy-to-read window showing the same report with the section headings highlighted. Inside that window you can copy it again, share it (on phones that support sharing), download it as a text file, or close it. The 911 note stays visible, and everything is available in English and Spanish. On phones the app no longer jumps straight to the share menu in place of the report; you always see the report now, and sharing is a button you tap when you want it.

A small header tweak. The ResponderTX logo is a touch larger so the wordmark reads more clearly, and the subtitle now says "Live Hazard Awareness" (the "for Texas" was dropped to keep it short and clean). Nothing about how the board works has changed, and the safety colors and 911 guidance are unchanged.

Live teams can now be more than search and rescue. When you create a team you pick its type: Search and Rescue (unchanged, still the default), Response for active-incident first responders, Recovery for post-incident cleanup, or Community for volunteer support. Each type offers its own list of member roles (for example Fire / Rescue, Cleanup, or Shelter), and the K9 name and skills fields stay only on search and rescue teams. A team's type is chosen once, when it is created. Everything already in use keeps working exactly as before: existing teams and saved member profiles are treated as search and rescue, and the 911 guidance is unchanged.

A visual polish to the header logo. The ResponderTX wordmark at the top-left is now larger and easier to read, and the tagline "Live Hazard Awareness for Texas" shows once instead of appearing twice (it had been baked into the logo art and also repeated as a line beneath it). Nothing about how the board works has changed, and all the safety colors and the 911 guidance stay exactly as before.

A visual refresh, not a change to how the board works. The app is now ResponderTX, with a new logo in the header, a navy-and-red color scheme drawn from the brand palette, and the tagline "Live Hazard Awareness for Texas." The browser tab and home-screen icons now use the new logo, and the interface fonts are bundled with the app so they load even on a weak connection. The colors that carry safety meaning (flood-severity levels, alert levels, and the green all-clear) are unchanged, and the 911 guidance stays exactly as before.

Two polish fixes. The map layers menu no longer shows a stray horizontal scrollbar: long camera labels now wrap neatly inside the panel instead of running off the edge. A board-wide punctuation pass also replaced the heavy em-dashes in the interface text (English and español) with cleaner separators, so labels, tooltips, and the legend read a little tidier. Nothing about how the board works has changed.

New live cameras from El Paso. You can now watch the City of El Paso's international-bridge cameras (the Paso del Norte, Santa Fe, Stanton, and Ysleta-Zaragoza crossings over the Rio Grande) live on the map. Turn them on under Cameras in the layers menu; like the other camera layers they are off by default. They open as live video in the same viewer the TxDOT highway cameras already use (with a LIVE badge), and every camera is credited to the City of El Paso. Because the operator occasionally renames its streams, the camera list is re-checked whenever it is rebuilt so only working cameras are shown.

Bug fixes and internal cleanup. The ☰ Filters button no longer shows a count for the distance filter when your device doesn’t have a GPS fix: the distance filter can’t hide anything without a location, so the button now stays quiet and agrees with the “✓ All clear” feed instead of contradicting it. Behind the scenes: the camera image proxy now cleanly rejects malformed camera-source names instead of erroring out, and the tool that rebuilds the camera list now refuses to publish an empty camera layer if an upstream source changes shape or goes dark (it stops with an error instead of silently dropping a whole layer of cameras). Some duplicate and unused internal code was also removed. No visible change to how the board works.

Bug fix. The positive “✓ All clear” message on an empty feed (added in v0.97.14) now shows correctly when you’ve set a distance filter but the board doesn’t have a GPS fix. The distance filter only takes effect once your location is known, so with no fix it isn’t actually hiding anything, but the feed was still showing the flat “No notices match the current filters” line instead of the all-clear. It now reads as all-clear in that case, and still shows the filter wording whenever a filter (type, county, search, time window, distance with a fix, or In-view) is genuinely what’s hiding notices. Also some behind-the-scenes cleanup: the team-relay endpoints now share one response helper and an unused map handle was removed, with no change to how the board behaves.

Cameras are now organized as independent, per-source layers you can turn on one at a time instead of a single all-or-nothing toggle. Alongside the TxDOT road cameras you can now enable City of Austin city cameras (817 public-domain arterial/intersection views), ATX Floods low-water-crossing flood cameras (26 creek-crossing cams built for exactly the “is this crossing under water” question), and Houston TranStar cameras (963, including the Galveston-Bolivar ferry), and the USGS river/flood-gage cameras now cover the whole state, so Houston, Dallas-Fort Worth and Laredo gage cams show up instead of being clipped to Central Texas. Each source keeps its own map marker, source credit and share link, and every camera layer stays off until you turn it on.

An empty feed now reads as good news. When there are no active notices and you haven't applied a restricting filter, the feed shows a positive all-clear message ("✓ All clear: no active notices right now. Quiet is good; stay ready.") instead of the flat "No notices match the current filters." The filter wording still appears when a type, county, search, time-window, distance, or In-view filter is actually what's hiding notices, so you're never misled into thinking the board is clear when it isn't. English y español.

Light mode is now the default, with the Streets basemap: the board opens in the sunlight-readable light theme instead of dark, which reads better on a phone in the field. Dark is still one tap away via the ☀️/🌙 control in the ⋮ menu, and if you switch it, your choice sticks. A one-time reset clears the theme that was silently saved for every prior visitor so the new light default actually takes effect on your next load. Nothing else changed.

Road reopenings now have their own layer. When a closure clears the live TxDOT feed, the board drops a green ✓ "recently reopened" marker. Until now those rode along on the road-closures layer and piled up on the map. They now live on a separate "Road reopenings" layer, nested under Live road closures in the layers sheet and off by default: turning closures on no longer turns reopenings on, so it takes one deliberate tap to show them. The markers are also limited to flood-related roads, so generic pavement or debris clearances no longer clutter the recovery view, and they still age off on their own (off the map after 12h, kept 7 days in the list). The choice travels in shared links as ?reopen=1 and shows a dismissible pill when it is on. No other layers or behavior change. English y español.

Bug fix. The "USGS stage" raw-gauge fallback layer kept re-appearing at the top-left of the map even after you closed it. When the live NWPS gauge feed goes quiet for more than ~15 minutes the board auto-shows the USGS raw-stage layer as a stand-in, but it was re-adding that layer on every refresh tick, so the moment you dismissed its pill it popped right back. Now it offers the fallback once when the feed goes stale, remembers when you dismiss it (via the pill ✕ or the layers sheet) and leaves it off until the live feed recovers, then re-arms the one-time offer only for a genuinely new outage. No other layers or behavior change.

Two small presentation cleanups. On the Team tab, the "call 911: this is situational awareness, not a dispatch system" line no longer appears twice: the board's persistent footer already shows that warning on every tab, so the redundant copy in the create and join views is gone (the footer disclaimer stays put). And on the Alerts list, each flood warning's "text" button now sits at the top-right of the card in the header row, aligned the same way the gauge cards put their NWPS link, so it reads as a consistent per-card action instead of trailing the expires time. Clicking it still opens the readable alert text. Desktop and phone behave the same. English y español.

Tap the "rising to major" chip and the board now makes the gauges in question unmistakable: it flies the map to frame exactly those gauges at a sensible zoom, plays a brief attention pulse on each of their markers so your eye lands on the right dots, and opens the Gauges tab with their rows scrolled into view and flashed. The "MAJOR gauges" chip does the same for symmetry. If you have the gauge layer turned off the map pulse is skipped, but the list still reveals the rows. Desktop and phone behave the same. English y español.

Two phone-first presentation tweaks for the field. When you are in a live team, the Team tab now jumps to the far left of the tab bar (first, ahead of Feed) so your crew view is the first thing under your thumb; it slides back to its normal last spot the moment you leave the team. And the Alerts list is denser on phones: trimmed padding and wasted vertical space around each alert card so more warnings fit on screen at once, with nothing dropped: the county and coordinates line, the readable alert link, and the severity color all stay, and tap targets stay big enough to hit. Desktop is unchanged. English y español.

Security hardening for live teams: a viewer is now truly watch-only. Before, the private id a member used to publish its own location doubled as its public name in the roster, so anyone with the team link could copy it and impersonate that member: moving their pin, dropping or clearing markers as them, or removing them from the team. Each person now carries two ids: a public one used only to show and follow them on the map, and a separate secret one, kept only on their own device, that signs their updates. The secret is never shown to anyone else, so a viewer can no longer act as a member. Nothing changes in how you create, join, or use a team. This release also adds the first automated tests for the team relay. A backend redeploy is required for the fix to take effect. English y español.

Live team sharing gets a real home. It moves out of the cramped ⋮ menu overlay into its own first-class "Team" tab alongside Feed, Alerts, Gauges, Social, and Resources. When you are not in a team the tab shows roomy create-a-team and join-by-link controls; once you are in, it shows the roster (with each member's type, specialty, K9 name, and status), your own prominent status control, and the team controls. Joining is now unmistakable: a primary "Join & share my location" and a secondary "Join as viewer (watch only)", with your call-sign field right above them. Members get a Waze-style drop-a-pin button in the bottom-right of the map to drop a shared waypoint, hazard, or search-area marker at the map center. Every control has a comfortable phone-sized tap target. The handle help no longer warns against your legal name, and the K9 skills list drops "HRD" (cadaver already covers it). Team map markers keep showing on the map no matter which tab you are on, and the board works normally for anyone who never opens the Team tab. English y español.

A LAN-only command view lands: the master oversight panel. On the ops board (never the public mirror) it lists every active team at once (with each team's name, how many are on it, and its makeup: K9 handlers vs ground, specialties, and who is in-field, on standby, or unavailable), plots all members on the map, and gathers every viewer across all teams into one roster. It is read-only oversight and refreshes on its own. Access is gated by a secret admin token held only on the server, so teams stay private: without the token nothing can be listed. English y español.

Team sharing gains a search-and-rescue member model. When you join a team you can now say who you are on the ground: a K9 handler (with your dog's name and skill tags like HRD, trailing, or cadaver) or a ground member with a specialty (searcher, medical, drone-ops, comms, and more), plus a status of in-field, standby, or unavailable. Your type and status show in the roster and right on your map marker, and you can change your role, type, or status any time after joining with one tap. Members can also drop shared team markers (waypoint, hazard, or search-area pins with a short label) that everyone on the team sees on the map and any member can clear; they are private to the team and never saved. When creating a team you can set the current map view as the team's default area so members land there. A new roster panel surfaces the nearest hospital and nearest veterinary to the team area from OpenStreetMap (honestly labeled: not verified as a trauma center or 24h emergency vet, so call ahead). English y español.

Live team location sharing is now reachable from the main board: tap ⋮ (More) → 🧭 Team. From there you can create a private team (the board shows a shareable link and a scannable QR code of it, so a teammate can join just by pointing their phone camera at your screen) or join an existing team by pasting its link or code. If you are already in a team, it reopens your roster. The feature stays private and opt-in exactly as before (unguessable link, call-sign handle, share-or-just-watch), and the board looks and works normally for anyone who never opens the Team menu. Runs on the secure site, where location sharing works. English y español.

Drive Mode now keeps your location fresh while you drive: once you tap ⌖ Locate, it quietly re-checks your position about every 30 seconds and re-ranks the nearest-hazards list by distance from where you actually are, with a small 'auto-updating · last fix Xs ago' note under the header. It only runs after you opt in by tapping Locate, and it stands down the moment you leave Drive Mode or switch away from the tab, so there is no background battery drain. English y español.

Map polish: the 'road reopened' marker is now a muted green ✓ recovery badge instead of a plain circle, so it no longer looks like an alert marker or blends into the other round markers on the map, with a matching legend entry. And small map markers (reopened roads, cameras, storm reports, USGS gauges) are easier to tap on a phone: each gets a larger invisible touch area while still pointing at the exact spot. English y español.

New: live team location sharing (opt-in, private). Open a team's link to join as a member (you share your live position as a colored marker with a Garmin-style breadcrumb trail, refreshed every ~15s) or as a viewer, who watches the team without sharing any location. Every team is private to its own unguessable link; there are no public teams and nothing is indexed. Handles are call signs (min 4 characters), never accounts or personal info, and one tap stops sharing and drops you. Positions live only in an ephemeral relay, auto-expire, and are never saved to the archive. Flag-gated behind ?team= on the secure site; the flood board is unchanged without it. English y español.

Mobile card polish: on the feed, 'navigate' and 'copy coords' are now real tappable buttons, and a card's source shows as a compact link (platform name or bare domain with a ↗) instead of a full raw URL. The citation is unchanged, just tidier. Alert cards wrap cleanly on narrow screens: the event name keeps its EMERGENCY flag, the sent/until times stay whole, and the 'text' reader is a right-aligned chip. Badges like CURATED and 'stale · re-verify' now render to one consistent size. English y español.

Tapping an alert card now flashes that alert's outline on the map and drops a pulsing ping at its center, so when several warnings overlap (like three Devils River reaches in Val Verde) you can see exactly which one you tapped. Falls back to a ping at the alert's center when its polygon is not drawn.

Alert cards no longer look like duplicates: each now names the specific river reach on the second line, so three 'Val Verde, TX' Flood Warnings read as Devils River at Cauthorn Ranch near Juno, at Bakers Crossing, and at Pafford Crossing. And the 'text' link now opens the full alert in-app (headline, description, what-to-do, in-effect-until), cited to the issuing NWS office, instead of dumping you at a raw API JSON page, with a raw-data link kept for provenance. Alert search now matches the river name too.

Honesty fix: the 'Am I at risk?' address card used to say your address is never transmitted, but placing the pin sends your typed address to OpenStreetMap's geocoder to turn it into coordinates. Corrected the wording (English + Spanish) to say exactly that: the address goes to the OpenStreetMap geocoder and is not stored, logged, or shared beyond that one lookup. No change to how it works.

The board is no longer an installable home-screen app: phones will stop prompting you to add or install it. Installed that way it had no way to update itself and could get stuck on a stale version; a normal browser tab always refreshes. Open it in your browser as usual. If you already added it to your home screen, delete that icon and reopen from the browser.

One radar timeline: the live radar scrub and the forecast-model scrub are now a single bar: observed radar to the left of a NOW divider, the HRRR model future (+1h to +18h) as an amber dashed segment to the right. Scrub straight across NOW and the map cross-fades between observed echoes and the model future with no blank frame; play loops past → NOW → forecast. Forecast stepping got the replay radar's buffering fix (preload hidden, cross-fade ~350ms, prefetch ahead, skip unchanged hours) and the model tiles render at double resolution with light softening so HRRR's blocky cells read like normative radar. The future zone keeps its amber dashed dress and FORECAST MODEL badge, the +18h cap stays, and the bar hides during historical replay. English y español.

Visual QA fixes: the ops-chat button no longer covers the replay bar's NOW/✕ on desktop and the forecast scrub stays clear of it at every width; Spanish threat counters wrap instead of cutting off mid-phrase ("medidores nivel MAYOR", "cerca del récord histórico"); popups opened from search or a card tap no longer slide under the top of the map on phones; phone feed cards keep their line ends clear of the floating controls; the chat unread badge caps at 99+ and a new device no longer opens to a three-digit badge; "crecida" replaces "onda" as the Spanish term for a river wave; and road popups drop TxDOT's leading "-" artifact.

Forecast radar: a new 'Forecast radar (HRRR model)' layer shows where NOAA's HRRR weather model expects storms over the next 18 hours, with an amber dashed scrub (+1h to +18h, play loop) and a persistent FORECAST MODEL badge so a model's guess can never be mistaken for observed radar; it can run alongside live radar (the two scrub bars stack), hides honestly during historical replay, refreshes with each hourly model run, and credits NOAA HRRR via Iowa Environmental Mesonet. Also hardened: a crafted ?base= link value can no longer confuse the basemap picker. English y español.

Defect-fix release: a bad ?theme= link can no longer break the board (and a previously saved bad value repairs itself); fast camera switching can't mislabel imagery anymore; layer pills honor the replay lock like the layer picker; the point-check card says 'LIVE data' when the map is replaying history; the replay's dashed river line is relabeled 'river peaks in sequence' (timing-based, not verified direction); replay radar/rainfall no longer stalls on a slow pre-loaded step; degraded boots still learn of updates; and shared links / update reloads now keep your Radar, Cameras, USGS, crossings, and inundation layers on. English y español.

Calmer map, honest details: the area quick-jump row now rests as one compact chip naming the area you're viewing: tap it to expand the presets, pick one and it collapses right back, and if you pan away it reads 'custom view' instead of an area it no longer shows. The map-layers and share buttons get clean icons matched to the zoom controls in both dark and light themes. Road closure popups now show the segment's length and explain the limits: a Closure is barricaded across the full listed stretch, while flooding/damage reports may be localized within it. English y español.
Radar scrub label simplified to 'now' (dropped the free-feed caveat.

Replay radar and rainfall now glide instead of flashing: each time-step preloads its tiles invisibly and cross-fades in (~350ms), frames whose radar/rainfall bucket hasn't changed skip the redraw entirely, scrubbing waits for your thumb to settle before fetching, and play prefetches the next step ahead: no more blank-map redraws mid-replay.

Playback now tells the truth everywhere: shelters, cameras, and other live-only layers disappear while you replay the past, and notices, cut-off circles, crossings, and storm reports appear only at the moments they actually existed. Rainfall joins the replay from the archived MRMS record: pick 1h/24h/48h/72h and it follows the timeline hour by hour. The story captions add evacuations, cut-off areas, shelter openings, and critical notices, and an optional archival-photo hook can pin sourced, credited photos to the timeline. English y español.

The map and the lists now work as one product: every notice popup on the map gains “Open in feed →” and every gauge popup “Open in gauges list →”: tap it and the matching card scrolls into view with a brief highlight pulse. A new “In view” chip on the Feed and Gauges tabs scopes the list to whatever the map is showing, with a live count that updates as you pan; turn it off to get the full list back. Cards that fly the map now carry a small 📍 so the affordance is visible. English y español.

Playback now rebuilds the full incident picture: road closures replay on the map frame by frame (archived TxDOT snapshots where we have them, honestly labeled as reconstructed from posted closure times before the archive began), flooding gauges call out prominently: majors draw twice as large, a colored ring pulses when a gauge changes category, and the 5 most significant gauges carry always-visible name + stage labels; and when the data shows a crest translating down a river (Devils, Llano, Pecos, Nueces chains tonight) an animated dashed arrow traces it between gauges with a “crest moving downstream” tag. English y español.

Slimmer chrome, same signal: the plain-language situation line is now a thin one-line strip (tap it to read the full sentence), and the bottom 911 notice fits on a single row at every width: tap it any time to reopen the full safety notice, or tap the underlined version number for What's New. Also fixed: the headline no longer says a river wave is “rising” when the gauge is actually receding: the direction word now follows the live trend. English y español.

Rainfall is now one layer, not two. Turn on Rainfall and pick the accumulation window (1h, 24h, 48h, or 72h) with the new chips right in the map legend; your pick sticks for the session, and old “Rain 1h”/“Rain 24h” links still open the right view. Also, by popular demand the 🔗 Share button is back at your fingertips: it now sits on the map just below the 🗂 layer button (and stays in the ⋮ menu too). English y español.

A friendlier layer picker: the old cramped checkbox list is gone. Tap the 🗂 button on the map (or the ＋ in the layer pills) to open a grouped sheet (Water, Rain & radar, Roads, Alerts & reports, History) where every layer has a plain name, a one-line "what is this", and a big toggle. Phones get a thumb-friendly bottom sheet; desktops a compact panel. OFFICIAL/CURATED badges make the two crossings layers unmistakable, a segmented Dark/Light/Streets row switches the basemap, ⏮ Playback opens the timeline, and ↺ Reset restores the default view and full-area framing in one tap. Everything is in English y español.

A tidier top bar: the header now shows just search 🔍, Drive 🚗, refresh ⟳, and a single ⋮ button: Share, light/dark theme, EN/ES language, and the "?" Legend now live inside the ⋮ menu with full labels, so phones (and desktops) get real breathing room. The corner time stamp slimmed down to a small green dot + time; hover it to see when the next auto-refresh lands. Buttons are also a touch bigger for gloved thumbs, and the header is no taller than before.

835 more TxDOT cameras, the snapshot-only ones: many city-street cams (most of Houston, Fort Worth, Dallas, Beaumont, El Paso) have no live video feed, only a photo that refreshes about every 2 minutes. They now appear on the camera layer as dashed/muted 📷 markers so you can tell them from live cams; opening one shows the latest photo with a SNAPSHOT badge, when it was captured, a ↻ refresh button, and the usual STALE warning if the image is over 45 minutes old. If a snapshot can’t be fetched, the viewer says so plainly.

The board now updates itself, gracefully: when a new version ships, the app waits until you're not mid-anything (no open panel or viewer, no playback, no recent touch, data refresh settled), shows a quiet 4-second "reloading" notice with a Later button, then reloads into the new build with your exact view restored (map position, tab, filters, basemap, theme), confirmed by a small ✓ toast that opens What's New. Hidden tabs update when you return to them, an unsent LAN chat draft survives the reload, and lag-safe guards mean it can never reload-loop. The ⬆ chip remains for an immediate manual reload.

First-run help & one search box: new visitors get a 3-panel intro (after the 911 notice): the map is live hazards, the feed is verified notices, long-press checks a point and ⏮ replays the event; a new "?" Legend button in the header explains every symbol (gauge colors, STALE, map markers, threat chips, OFFICIAL vs CURATED badges, aging, playback, USNG) in EN/ES; and one header 🔍 search now finds places/addresses, gauges by name or ID, typed "lat, lon", and R-### cards; a place result flies the map and drops the point-check card there. The separate 🔍 ID button folded into the search.

Playback mobile quality pass: taps on the playback bar no longer zoom the map: every control (scrub, ⏪/⏩, chips, speed, play, NOW, ✕, caption, HUD, chapter ticks, plus the radar scrub) now blocks tap-through and browser double-tap zoom; the scrub gets a fat 28px thumb on a full-width 44px-tall touch row on phones with ≥44px step/play/speed buttons; choosing 7d/14d now shows the full requested window with the span before the archive's Jul 17, 10:31 AM CT birth hatched out plus a one-time 'Archive begins…' note (no frames are ever faked); entering playback auto-sets 0.5× speed and minimizes the bottom pane, restored on NOW/close; the sheet handle and chat button no longer collide with the open bar.

UX quick wins: (1) long-press or right-click anywhere on the map to inspect that point: a compact card shows the plain-language risk read, nearest gauge, active alert, nearest closed crossing/notice, and the USNG grid (tap to copy) without the map moving under you; (2) a one-sentence plain-language headline now tops the threat strip ("Asherton MAJOR flood cresting near record · Devils River wave rising at Pafford Crossing…"), tap to expand on phones; (3) when a non-default map layer is on (radar, rain, cameras…), slim pills over the map name it: tap a pill to turn the layer off, ＋ opens all layers; the row disappears when nothing extra is on.

Playback story mode: playback now tells the story of the event. Archived NWS warning polygons (official products from IEM's storm-based warning archive) roll in and fade on the map at the exact times they were valid, in the same colors as live alerts, with an 'NWS warning archive via IEM' label; a tap-to-pause caption strip above the timeline narrates gauge rises/falls, crests with crest-of-record context (Asherton crests 31.48 ft · 95% of the 1935 record), warning issue/expiry, and road reopenings; play now defaults to a slower ~2 frames/sec with a 0.5×/1×/2×/4× speed control and ⏪/⏩ one-frame step buttons, and at slow speeds markers glide between frames (a visual transition only; the timestamp always shows the real frame time); a one-line HUD reads gauges in flood by category, warnings active, and the top mover (tap to expand). Live alert polys swap out while replaying and snap back on NOW.

New map layer: road & river cameras. 656 TxDOT live traffic cams (HLS video, LIVE badge, not recorded) + 9 USGS HIVIS river cams (15-min stills with capture time, STALE badge past 45 min): off by default, clustered 📷 markers, one-tap viewer that closes with ✕, tap-outside, or Escape. Drive Mode shows the 2 nearest cams; a gauge within 2 km of a river cam (Wimberley/Blanco) links straight to it. A camera shows one spot at one moment: verify before routing.
Gauge section headers trimmed: 'Forecast to flood' and 'Rising' drop the pre-position phrasing.

Historical playback (the flagship ask): a collapsible bottom timeline bar over the map replays the event over 3d / 7d / 14d: drag the scrub or press ▶ (~8 fps) to watch gauge conditions rise and recover, with ▲ chapter marks at each major crest (tap to jump), a CT timestamp readout, and a NOW button that snaps back to live instantly; gauges replay from a new committed archive (data/history.json, one frame per 15 min, stale sensors badged) and radar replays from IEM's archived NEXRAD tiles when the radar layer is on; alerts, roads, crossings, and reports have no per-frame archive and stay live; the bar says so, and an amber PLAYBACK pill plus a dimmed threat strip make historical frames unmistakable. Open it from the ⏮ pill by the map controls, ▶ Playback in ⋯ More, or ?playback=1.

Readability pass: swept unnecessary em-dashes out of all on-screen text (EN and ES) in favor of plain commas, colons, and the board's usual · separator, and switched SITREP/AAR text exports to plain hyphens for radio/SMS paste; the 📊 Crest summary view is now one tap away via a chip at the top of the Gauges tab (still in ⋯ More too); the header subtitle is now "Life Safety & Response Feed" (ES: "Seguridad de vida y respuesta").

Source tagging (official vs curated): signals with ambiguous provenance now carry a small badge: blue OFFICIAL for machine-fed authoritative feeds (NWS, NWPS, IEM, TxDOT/TDEM, TxGIO, USGS) and amber CURATED for operator-maintained data (notice cards, tracked crossings), on feed cards, crossing lists and popups, road-layer popups, and the curated-driven threat chips, with tooltips explaining each; SITREP lines gain (official)/(curated) suffixes so provenance survives copy-paste.

Event crest summary (after-action part 1): a new read-only crest summary view (⋯ More → 📊 Crest summary, or ?view=summary) shows every gauge that reached flood stage this event with its peak stage, category, peak time (CT), in-flood window, and crest-of-record comparison (Asherton peaked at 95% of the 1935 record), built from the board's committed 15-minute snapshot archive by a new release-time generator; frozen-sensor peaks are badged STALE and gauges still in flood are marked ongoing.

Recovery posture, part 1: the board now tracks road closures that clear from the live DriveTexas feed and surfaces them as ✓ REOPENED: green markers on the map, a 'Recently reopened roads' list in Resources, and low-priority ✓ rows in Drive Mode (aging out of view after 12h, kept 7 days). And when the AO goes fully quiet (no in-AO alerts, no gauges in flood, no closures) the threat strip shows a calm 'No active flood threat in the AO, monitoring N gauges' line instead of sitting empty.

Under-the-hood maintenance: the app's single 3,200-line script was split into six modules (core, map, sources, panels, board, boot) to keep development manageable, with no visible or behavioral changes.

Bug-fix release from a code audit: idle clients now repaint aging badges and crossing status flips (without losing your scroll position), map-popup source links are gated to http(s) only, overlapping refreshes are queued instead of racing, the LAN server rejects cross-origin POSTs and stops serving repo internals, and one bad feature in a feed can no longer blank that whole source for the cycle.

The map attribution footer now collapses to a small tap-to-open ⓘ instead of a full-width bar: it was crowding the legend on landscape/short phones. Tap ⓘ to see the full credits (OSM, CARTO, Leaflet, TxDOT DriveTexas / TDEM); they're preserved (required by the map providers and our citation rule) but no longer sit over the map.

Fix: the expanded map legend was clipped on landscape phones / when the sheet is expanded: too tall for the short map (worse after v0.76.1 added the Roads rows), cutting off the title and bottom rows. The open legend now caps its height and scrolls (title-first) instead of clipping, and scrolling it no longer zooms the map. Portrait and desktop unchanged.

Drive Mode now uses the live road-closure data: its nearest-hazard glance list includes the TDEM DriveTexas closures/flooded/damaged roads (⛔/🌊/⚠) ranked by distance to the closest point on each closure, alongside crossings, life-safety notices, and major gauges, so a driver sees the nearest flooded/closed road, not just gauges and curated crossings.

Road-hazard layer now excludes construction-driven closures: TxDOT codes some construction shutdowns as 'Closure'/'Damage' (not 'Construction'), so they slipped past our condition filter and showed as red closure lines; a server-side description filter now drops 'closed due to construction' entries (4 of 102 in the AO this cycle) so the board stays flood-relevant. Null-safe: a closure with no description is still shown.

Map legend now keys the v0.76.0 road-hazard line colors: a new 'Roads (DriveTexas)' section shows red = Road CLOSED, magenta = Flooded / high water, amber = Road damage, with colors pulled straight from ROAD_COND so they can't drift from the map. Small usability fix so the live road-closure lines are self-explanatory in the field.

Live road-hazard source swap: the 'Road closures / high water (TxDOT)' layer now pulls from the live TDEM DriveTexas API instead of the TxDOT HCRS_CC service, which had been frozen since Aug 2020 so the layer never showed live closures: ~100 flood-relevant hazard lines (Closure/Flooding/Damage) now render live in the AO, including the destroyed Nueces River bridge on FM 481; all fields remapped to the DriveTexas schema (full-word conditions, ISO end_time aging, numeric detour_flag) and attributed to TxDOT DriveTexas / TDEM. New 'Low-water crossings (locations · not live status)' overlay (off by default, lazy-loaded, canvas-rendered) plots 3,753 TxGIO crossing LOCATIONS across the AO; every popup and the label make clear it is a static inventory with NO live flood status. Both layers stay out of the offline cache and all KPI/threat counts and degrade to empty if their feed is down.

Mobile-landscape usability fix: a phone held sideways used to fall into the full desktop layout (its 844-932px landscape width cleared the app's only WIDTH breakpoint) and showed no feed cards on a viewport with no vertical room. A new height-based landscape query now puts phones held sideways into a map-favoring side-by-side split: compact icon header, a narrow scrollable feed sidebar, the map filling ~60%, and a short one-line 911 footer that still taps open to the full notice; tablets and all portrait layouts are unchanged. The map now also re-tiles cleanly on device rotation.

Live TxDOT DriveTexas road layer: 'Road closures / high water (TxDOT)' toggle (on by default) fetches HCRS_CC flood-relevant conditions (Closed/Flood/Damage) over the AO, in-browser CORS, refreshes on cycle, ages out cleared closures via COND_END_TS, and degrades to an empty layer if the feed is down.
'New notice' intake button suppressed by default: code + form kept intact, revealed by ?intake=1 (same gating as ?risk=1 / ?notes=1); static hidden attr so no boot flash.

Map recency for flash-flood iconography: the alert-polygon draw loop skips any NWS alert whose expires time is in the past, so a stale/expired alert can no longer linger on the map after a failed refresh; alerts still open (expires in the future, any age) keep drawing, so an open FF EMERGENCY/WARNING is never hidden. New lsrMaxHours=24 hard cap on live storm-report (💧) markers: a report older than 24h routes to the existing aged/history layer instead of the live layer even when the window filter is wider: suppress, not delete.

Security + quality hardening pass: a crafted ?tab= URL param can no longer throw an invalid-selector exception that blanked the board on load; edge gauge-proxy rejects prototype-chain kinds cleanly; the cold-start snapshot no longer overwrites fresher live gauges in a race; gauge feed numbers are coerced and off-site links scheme-gated to http(s) as defense-in-depth; added nosniff / Referrer-Policy / X-Frame-Options headers; Escape now closes modals and the version tag is keyboard-focusable; the 'normal' gauge bucket labels stale sensors distinctly. (CSP deferred: the board loads many external tile/API origins and a strict policy could not be fully verified safe.)

Recency filter for dead gauges: a frozen sensor (West Nueces at Brackettville, obs stuck ~60h at MAJOR while its own forecast read no-flooding) no longer counts as an active flood gauge: a 12h staleness cutoff now suppresses stale readings from the in-flood KPI, threat strip, crest-wave, and record-watch, while the gauge stays visible and badged STALE (suppress, not delete). Fresh gauges are unaffected.

Mobile polish: the Feed tab's action-button strip (New notice / SITREP / Filters / ID / More) is condensed to a shorter, tighter row on phones, and the default bottom-sheet now cheats a 60/40 split so the map takes the dominant ~60% and the feed panel ~40%.

Extended gauge coverage west to the Pecos: the AO bounding box now runs to -102.0 (was -101.2), so tonight's Val Verde flood wave, Pandale Crossing (PDAT2, MAJOR forecast) and Langtry (LTRT2), shows as live gauge dots with hydrographs instead of sitting off the map's west edge. Added a 'Val Verde/Pecos' quick-jump chip and widened the 'Full AO' view to match.

Brand subtitle shortened + genericized: 'Hill Country flood event · live NWS/NOAA/USGS · community assistance feed' → 'First Responder & Life Safety Feed' (ES: 'Primeros respondedores y seguridad de vida'). The board is being built to manage multiple AOs and separate statewide events, so the header no longer names one single event.

NEW Spanish/English toggle (🌐 in the header): the app's own UI now speaks Spanish or English for the heavily Hispanic flood-affected area: tabs, the threat-to-life module, the safety modal, the 911 disclaimer, Drive Mode and the 'Am I at risk?' honesty box. Safety copy uses standard NWS/FEMA Spanish ('llame al 911', 'Dé la vuelta, no se ahogue', 'no es un sistema de despacho'); live NWS/NOAA/USGS gauge and alert data stays in English. Choice is remembered and ?lang=es deep-links. Also: 'Am I at risk?' is now hidden by default (still reachable at ?risk=1): this is a responder/public-info board, not a consumer address lookup

NEW '🏠 Am I at risk?' address check (header): type an address → geocoded to a point → a risk-glance card of the nearest river gauges (stage, category, forecast crest, trend) within 15 mi, any active NWS flood alert covering the point, and the nearest closed crossing / road notice, with one plain-language read line; flies the map and drops a 'YOUR PLACE' marker, and one tap saves the place for re-checking later. Guidance only: not a flood determination; it never says you're safe; your address is used only on your device to place the pin and is never logged or transmitted

NEW street-level flood inundation overlay (layer control, off by default): NOAA/NWPS National Water Model inundation extent (the 'which roads go under' picture) shown as a translucent blue extent when you zoom to street level; labelled a MODELED estimate (experimental), not observed conditions, cites NOAA/NWPS and updates hourly
Resize control moved off the panel strip onto a small floating ▲/↕/▼ pill (bottom-right, above the chat button): zero panel space, and Minimize is now a true full-screen map
Map legend now collapses to a small pill on desktop too (click to expand): it was covering the busiest corner of the map (Eagle Pass/Del Rio cluster)

Bottom-sheet fixes: the Min/Half/Full buttons are now a small pill (not oversized), and the sheet no longer hijacks the 911 footer or version tag: the map flexibly fills the space so Full/Half keep the footer at the bottom
Alerts tab now puts Hill Country AO alerts first and folds far-away TX warnings into an 'elsewhere' group (no more Big Bend warning on top); Drive Mode made discoverable (teal accent + one-time hint)
Mobile panel fix: explicit ▼ Min / ↕ Half / ▲ Full buttons (always visible) replace the hard-to-find grabber: jump straight to any size, always easy to bring the panel back

Mobile bottom-sheet: tap the grabber to slide the feed/alerts panel between peek (map full-screen), half, and full (panel covers the map for full-screen scroll): the map is finally fully viewable on phones

Mobile header decluttered: Refresh/Share/Drive/theme are icon-only on phones (one uncramped row, big tap targets), the 'next in' countdown is hidden, and the app name gets room; desktop keeps full labels

Usability tweaks: Gauges tab opens to the gauge list (Crest Wave now a collapsed toggle); offline-save is now a subtle ⬇ button (green when cached) and caches 2 deeper zoom levels so you can zoom in offline

NEW ⬇ Save map offline (bottom-left): caches the current view + one zoom deeper into IndexedDB so the basemap keeps drawing in canyon dead zones: works on plain LAN http, no Service Worker; basemap only, gauge/alert data still needs a connection
NEW public RSS feed (/feed.xml: emergencies, forecast crests, notices) + subscribable crest calendar (/crests.ics); follow the event in any reader or calendar app, no account needed
NEW full-screen hydrograph (tap ⤢ in a gauge popup, or ?hydro=<lid>): 24h observed + NWPS forecast trace, flood-stage bands, crest-of-record line, now marker, dated axes

NEW 🚗 Drive Mode: full-screen big-type nearest-hazards glance list (crossings, life-safety notices, MAJOR gauges) ranked by distance + bearing from your GPS, built for the truck; tap a row to fly there

NEW low-water crossing tracker: curator-maintained closed/caution crossings as a color-coded map layer + Resources list, each stamped with update time (stale after 12h) and 'verify before routing' + DriveTexas link
Shared links now unfurl with a branded card + description on X/Facebook/iMessage/Slack (Open Graph + Twitter meta); evergreen by design so cached social images never show stale counts
NEW 🌊 Crest-Wave tracker (Gauges tab): each river's gauges in crest-arrival order with forecast ETAs: 'when does the wave reach my town' for the multi-day downstream progression; taps fly the map

NEW crest-of-record watch: gauges forecast near/above their all-time NWPS record crest are flagged in the threat module, on gauge cards, and in SITREP: honest margin-to-record, never an invented 'record-breaking' claim
Your view is now remembered: feed + alert filters, sort, aged toggle, and active tab persist across hard refreshes and app updates (shared-link URLs still win for their load)
Product vet pass: shared radio-ID links (?fq=R-031) now fly the map to the card on load; multi-digit ID typing no longer wobbles through intermediate matches; full UAT of every feature passed
Radio-ID search: type R-031 (or tap 🔍 ID) and the board filters to the card AND flies the map to its pin; Field Notes hidden by default; deep links still work
Radio-speakable IDs on every feed card (R-036 style): tap to copy, shown in popups, prefixed on SITREP critical lines: 'flag R-036' now works over radio/SMS
Light theme retuned for sunlight: darker inks, stronger hairlines, and light-mode severity colors: ambers/oranges/grays no longer wash out on white
GPS feedback: locate taps and distance filters show a pulsing 'acquiring GPS fix…' chip until the fix lands or times out (20s): no more silent waiting
MRMS rainfall layers now show a color-scale legend on the map (ramp sampled from the live tiles, light→extreme): no more guessing what the colors mean in the field
Intake accepts typed coordinates: the lat/lon field is now editable: radio-relayed decimal coords parse, validate, pin, and pan the map; phones scroll the map into view when the form opens
NEW 🔗 Share button: one tap copies a link that reproduces your exact view: map position/zoom, active tab, Feed + Alerts filters, basemap, theme; phones get the native share sheet

Gauge graphs are now served from our own 3-min cache (Cloudflare edge on the public site, LAN server locally) with automatic direct-NOAA fallback: faster popups, far fewer NOAA hits during rate-limit windows

Calmer warnings + first-visit safety: snapshot bar only when the snapshot is ≥30 min old, warning bar dismissable, one-time 911 / DO-NOT-SELF-DEPLOY acknowledgment modal, degraded note names the failing feed
NEW 📍 Field Notes: community/responder annotation flyout: drop-pin map notes with categories, comment threads, general posts, shareable ?note= deep links; the public mirror shows notes read-only

Command-area rework: threat strip is now a structured status module (aligned tiered stat rows, next-crest countdown, FF EMERG mini-chips), tabs never wrap their count badges, app renamed to just 'Responder TX', slimmer header: phones reclaim a big chunk of feed space
Nav package: merged zoom/locate bar (un-jumbled corner), AO quick-jump chips (Kerr, Uvalde/Frio, Sonora/Ozona, Cibolo, Full AO), tappable KPI tiles, 911 line pinned to phone viewport bottom
Stale-tab fix: open tabs now show a green 'Updated, tap to reload' chip when a newer build ships (no auto-reload); ticker slowed ~30% for readability

Radar suppression FIXED: frames were being painted over by the Streets base (z-index bug); radar now lives in its own pane above every base; opacity 0.75; past crests dropped from the next-crest chip

Cache fix for the public mirror (versioned assets + no-store data: fixes 'no data' on stale edge caches); NEW: RFC forecast-crest rings layer + clustered USGS raw-stage layer (224 sites, off by default); Sonora emergency EXTENDED to 1:15 PM
Notices, not requests: no manual status/archive management: curator resolves via data, resolved auto-suppresses, everything ages out; Feed tab, Active notices tile, New notice intake

PUBLIC LAUNCH: live at https://responder.rfxn.com (Cloudflare Pages, SSL) + open source at github.com/rfxn/responder-tx; HTTPS unlocks geolocation/clipboard/PWA install; public mirror is read-only (chat send stays LAN)

UX-audit dangerous fixes: stale-data warning bar, guarded status changes (confirm resolve, explicit reopen), alerts-first Alerts tab with red emergency badge, phone declutter (tiles hidden, 1-line disclaimer, FAB clearance), card-tap map linkage; 8 vetted live resources added (iSTAT, DriveTexas, SARiverFlood, CSR activation)
